deferred_items.description: Creates a detailed cost breakdown for every item on the menu — coffee, food, pastries, drinks — so the cafe knows exactly what each product costs to make, what margin it generates, and whether its price is set correctly. This block is the financial foundation upon which all pricing, profitability, and cost control decisions are built.
deferred_items.title: Menu Item Costing Calculator

deferred_items.description: Analyses and optimises the cost of all packaging — cups, lids, sleeves, bags, boxes, napkins, cutlery, and delivery containers — ensuring the cafe minimises this significant cost line without compromising quality, customer experience, or sustainability commitments.
deferred_items.title: Packaging Cost Optimisation

deferred_items.description: Establishes an Instagram-first social media strategy that builds brand awareness, drives footfall, and creates a visual identity for the cafe online. For most cafes, Instagram is the primary social platform where potential customers discover, evaluate, and decide to visit — this block ensures the cafe has an intentional, consistent presence rather than a sporadic, unfocused one.
deferred_items.title: Instagram & Social Strategy

deferred_items.description: Elevates the cafe's social media presence from consistent posting to strategic marketing excellence — using data-driven content optimisation, multi-platform coordination, paid amplification where appropriate, and community-building tactics that turn followers into regular customers and brand advocates.
deferred_items.title: Social Media Excellence

deferred_items.description: Ensures that all 14 major allergens are correctly identified in every menu item and clearly communicated to customers through compliant labelling. This block is the foundation of allergen safety and underpins legal compliance with UK food allergen regulations.
deferred_items.title: Allergen Identification & Labelling

deferred_items.description: Establishes a comprehensive allergen awareness culture across the entire cafe operation, encompassing training, auditing, and continuous improvement. This block brings together allergen documentation, labelling, and communication into a unified prevention programme that minimises the risk of allergen-related incidents.
deferred_items.title: Allergen Prevention Programme

deferred_items.description: Establishes a clear, enforced handwashing protocol specifying when, how, and for how long staff must wash their hands, with compliance monitoring to prevent pathogen transmission. This block is the foundation of personal hygiene in any food business and a key element inspected by EHOs.
deferred_items.title: Handwashing Protocol

deferred_items.description: Embeds hygiene as a core cultural norm within the cafe team, moving beyond checklists and compliance into a shared identity where cleanliness and food safety are points of pride. This block transforms hygiene from something staff are told to do into something they choose to do because they understand its importance.
deferred_items.title: Hygiene Culture System

deferred_items.description: Establishes a structured pre-shift team briefing — a daily five-to-ten-minute huddle that aligns the team on priorities, shares critical information, surfaces concerns, and sets the tone for the shift ahead.
deferred_items.title: Daily Team Briefing Protocol

deferred_items.description: Builds an open, psychologically safe communication culture where feedback flows freely in all directions — upward, downward, and peer-to-peer — ensuring issues are raised early, good work is recognised, and the team operates with transparency and mutual respect.
deferred_items.title: Communication Culture & Feedback

deferred_items.description: Creates a comprehensive, standardised opening checklist that ensures the cafe is fully prepared for trading every single day — covering safety checks, equipment start-up, stock readiness, cleanliness, and regulatory compliance so that nothing is missed regardless of who opens.
deferred_items.title: Opening Checklist

deferred_items.description: Creates a structured shift handover process that ensures critical information — outstanding tasks, equipment issues, customer complaints, stock shortages, and operational notes — transfers reliably between outgoing and incoming teams. This block prevents the information black holes that form at every shift change.
deferred_items.title: Shift Handover System

deferred_items.description: Builds genuine, mutually beneficial relationships between the cafe and its local community — residents, businesses, schools, organisations, and groups — positioning the cafe as an integral part of the neighbourhood rather than just another commercial outlet. This block transforms the cafe from a transaction point into a community hub.
deferred_items.title: Local Community Engagement

deferred_items.description: Develops the cafe's ability to create events, build collaborations with other brands and businesses, and generate earned media coverage that amplifies the cafe's visibility beyond its regular customer base. This block transforms the cafe from a passive business into an active cultural participant in the local scene.
deferred_items.title: Events, Collaborations & PR

deferred_items.description: Ensures the cafe has a clear, documented business plan that articulates where the business is going, how it will get there, and what success looks like. This block transforms the owner's ambitions from unspoken assumptions into a written strategy that guides every significant decision, from menu changes to capital investment to hiring.
deferred_items.title: Business Plan & Strategy

deferred_items.description: Establishes an annual strategic planning cycle that forces the cafe owner to step back from daily operations, review the year honestly, set priorities for the next 12 months, and create accountability for execution. This block prevents the drift that occurs when cafes run on autopilot, repeating the same patterns year after year without deliberate improvement.
deferred_items.title: Strategic Planning & Annual Review

deferred_items.description: Develops a succession plan that ensures the cafe can continue operating successfully if the owner steps back, retires, becomes incapacitated, or decides to sell. This block covers leadership development, operational documentation, business valuation awareness, ownership transition options, and the often-neglected emotional and practical preparation required for founder exit.
deferred_items.title: Succession Planning

deferred_items.description: Establishes a systematic industry benchmarking programme that compares the cafe's performance, practices, and positioning against competitors, industry averages, and best-in-class operators. This block provides frameworks for financial benchmarking, operational benchmarking, customer experience comparison, and strategic positioning analysis — ensuring the owner makes decisions based on market reality rather than assumptions.
deferred_items.title: Industry Benchmarking

deferred_items.description: Develops a strategic, data-driven approach to menu pricing that balances cost coverage, competitive positioning, customer value perception, and profit maximisation. This block replaces the common practice of pricing by copying competitors with a structured methodology grounded in the cafe's own cost data.
deferred_items.title: Menu Pricing Strategy

deferred_items.description: Establishes a structured, data-driven approach to growing cafe revenue beyond incremental gains. Ensures the business identifies, tests, and scales new revenue streams while protecting the core proposition that customers value.
deferred_items.title: Revenue Growth Strategy

deferred_items.description: Ensures the cafe extracts maximum value from its Point of Sale system by configuring it correctly, training all staff to use it fully, and leveraging its data and integration capabilities. Most cafes use less than 30% of their POS features, leaving substantial operational intelligence and efficiency gains untapped.
deferred_items.title: POS System Optimisation

deferred_items.description: Creates a strategic technology investment plan that aligns technology spending with business priorities, prevents ad-hoc purchases that create integration nightmares, and ensures every technology decision serves a clear operational or commercial objective. This block transforms technology from an expense to be minimised into an investment to be optimised.
deferred_items.title: Technology Roadmap

deferred_items.description: Establishes a structured daily preparation list system that ensures every shift begins with a complete, prioritised checklist of prep tasks — from food preparation and ingredient restocking to station setup and mise en place. This block eliminates the guesswork and memory dependence that causes prep failures, rushed mornings, and service breakdowns during peak hours.
deferred_items.title: Daily Prep List Protocol

deferred_items.description: Creates a single-view operational dashboard that consolidates daily KPIs — revenue, labour cost, waste, speed of service, customer count, and compliance checks — into a real-time pulse of cafe performance. This block ensures the manager never has to guess how the day is going and can intervene before small problems become expensive ones.
deferred_items.title: Operational Rhythm Dashboard

constraint_report.triage_message: This venue activated 204 items, but a team of 8 can realistically absorb ~11 concurrent changes (focused mode). The plan has been trimmed to 13 highest-priority items. 191 items deferred to a future phase.

Okay, here's everything I know about Kalvos from the beginning to today — the full picture.

---

Kalvos is a destination restaurant and boutique stay in Žemaičių Kalvarija, a small town in the Plungė district of Žemaitija. It's owned by Simona Gimbutienė and Rolandas Gimbutas. The concept is built around slowness, nature, family, and grounding. It's not a local café, not a generic countryside getaway — it's positioned as a real destination with emotional depth. The tagline energy is "a place to arrive, not just to eat."

The property has a restaurant, 9 boutique rooms, private dining with two large round tables, a garden, a greenhouse, and event spaces. The cuisine direction is Nordic-Baltic fusion — seasonal, local, ingredient-first, modern Lithuanian with depth. The address is Vienuolyno g. 2, Žemaičių Kalvarija.

You, Matas Paulinas, are the hospitality consultant running the entire pre-launch project through your company MPCM — Matas Paulinas Consulting & Management. Your role is comprehensive: brand coordination, operations structuring, vendor management, staff hiring strategy, physical branding, menu development, onboarding documentation, financial setup coordination — essentially everything that needs to happen between "we have a building" and "we are open."

---

**The brand.**

The brandbook was created with Nomeda from Brand Me. It's solid — a full design system with specific colors (dark green #2d3526, cream #eee9e3, olive #5a6b4f), typography (Dashiell Fine for headings, Figtree for body, Emily Austin for accent), the hills symbol as the core brand mark, and a full material language: blind deboss, letterpress, natural uncoated stock, recycled leather, linen, cork, brass, corten steel. The brand is tactile and quiet — nature should frame everything, not a billboard.

You built a comprehensive physical branding touchpoints guide covering 8 zones: restaurant table (menu, napkins, ceramics, coasters, candle holders, bill presenter, wine list), bar and drinks (cocktail napkins, ice stamp, custom labels, stirrers), arrival and reception (exterior signage, door detail, welcome card, business cards, wax seal, stationery), hotel room (key card, bed linen, bathrobes, amenities, soap, slippers, room directory, coffee set, water carafe, laundry bag), bathroom (hand towels, soap dispenser, amenity bottles, candle, mirror detail), staff and kitchen (aprons, waiter aprons, order pads, name pins, prep labels, kitchen towels), takeaway and gifts (preserves, gift vouchers, tote bag, gift box, postcards, matchboxes), and outdoor (garden signage, wayfinding, herb markers, outdoor blankets, event signage).

---

**The website.**

You built the full Kalvos website — structure and branded HTML/CSS front-end. Eight sections: home, our story, restaurant, stay, events, experience, visit, and reserve. It's ready for content population and deployment. The hero says "A place to arrive, not just to eat." The story section covers Simona and Rolandas, why Žemaičių Kalvarija, the philosophy. Restaurant shows seasonal menu preview and the breakfast/lunch/dinner/tasting structure. Stay highlights the 9 rooms. Events covers private dining, celebrations, workshops, retreats, garden events. There's a visit section with location, hours (Wednesday through Sunday), parking, FAQ. All coded, all branded, all waiting for final photography and confirmed operational details.

---

**The menus.**

You developed the full menu suite in early March. Four separate menus, all delivered as editable HTML files:

The bar menu — 8 signature cocktails with a botanical and foraged direction: Birch & Smoke (birch sap, mezcal, charred rosemary), The Greenhouse (gin, cucumber, elderflower), Forest Floor (rye whiskey, chanterelle syrup), Sea Buckthorn Spritz, Amber Negroni (Lithuanian gin, pine tincture), Meadow Sour (bourbon, meadowsweet), Kalvos Mule (house ginger-rhubarb), After the Rain (pear brandy, thyme). Six bar snacks with pairing recommendations. Three Lithuanian craft beers on tap — Dundulis pilsner, Sakiškių wheat ale, Genys dark lager. Bottled beer and cider, spirits, three non-alcoholic cocktails (Garden Tonic, Birch Fizz, Berry & Thyme Shrub), soft drinks including house lemonade and seasonal kombucha.

The tasting menu — €105, with wine pairing at €65 and non-alcoholic at €35. Ten courses: two snacks (pine salt cracker with smoked curd; beetroot chip with horseradish and roe), two cold courses (cured Baltic herring; smoked eel), five warm courses (spring onion with whey butter; porcini broth; pike perch; duck breast with beetroot and black currant; lamb shoulder with wild garlic), and sweet (birch sap granita, honey and rye dessert, petit fours). Each course has a paired wine — all natural, small-producer: Enderle & Moll, Gut Oggau, Sybille Kuntz, Domaine Labet, Claus Preisinger, Milan Nestarec, Domaine de la Borde, Dorogi Tokaji.

The à la carte — 5 snacks, 3 cold appetisers (beetroot tartare, smoked trout, cured Baltic herring), 3 warm appetisers (seasonal soup, porcini broth, spring onion), 3 mains in the €24-28 range (pike perch, slow-cooked pork shoulder, duck breast), 3 desserts (honey and rye, dark chocolate mousse, apple and quark), sweet bites for coffee.

The wine card — two pages. Page one: sparkling (Crémant d'Alsace, Prosecco Col Fondo, Champagne Laherte Frères), whites (Müller-Thurgau, Grüner Veltliner Gut Oggau, Riesling Sybille Kuntz, Savagnin Domaine Labet, Chenin Blanc Domaine Mosse, Chablis Pattes Loup), rosé (Blaufränkisch Claus Preisinger), orange and skin-contact (Ribolla Gialla Radikon, Furmint Dobogó). Page two: reds (Pinot Noir Nestarec, Trousseau Domaine de la Borde, Zweigelt Judith Beck, Gamay Marcel Lapierre Morgon, Nerello Mascalese Frank Cornelissen Susucaru, Côtes du Rhône Gramenon La Sagesse, Barolo Giovanni Rosso at €95), sweet wines (Tokaji Dorogi, Muscat de Rivesaltes Cazes), digestifs (grappa Marolo, calvados Dupont, Amaro Montenegro, Fernet-Branca, house Krupnikas, cognac Pierre Ferrand, pear eau de vie), coffee and tea. Glass range €7-12, bottles €34-95. Two price columns: glass and bottle.

Simona liked the menus. Rolandas still hasn't looked at them as of mid-March. The menus are now going to be bilingual — Lithuanian and English. Nomeda has been briefed and asked to design the physical menus from scratch using the brandbook and your content. Her task: layout, typography, paper selection, print preparation. The restaurant menu is A4 hardcover, leather or linen bound, blind deboss hills symbol, with seasonal inserts. The tasting menu is a single-page insert. The wine card is separate but same material language. The bar menu is lighter format — single card or folded, heavier stock.

---

**The team and operations.**

You built the entire operations structure documentation: job descriptions for kitchen, FOH, and admin positions, responsibility and reporting logic, onboarding process, work discipline and communication model, minimum SOPs, and daily routine structure. You created a FOH manager job posting — a really personal, values-driven text that talks about Kalvos as a home where the administrator is the host. The strategy was personal profile post first, then HR groups, then Stories, then Kalvos accounts. You created an HR cost dynamics tool for Rolandas to set salary ranges. All of this was delivered in early March. None of it has been confirmed as reviewed by the owners.

For the chef position, there are two candidates. Vidas — who was supposed to start but didn't because his current workplace has staff shortages. You had to contact him yourself because the owners weren't sharing information. He's still wrapping up his old job. Then there's Andrius — who nearly got lost because he sent a menu, nobody responded for a week, he assumed they weren't interested and decided to stay at his current restaurant. You called him directly, figured out what happened, told Simona, and she managed to recover the relationship. Both are confirmed to visit Kalvos on March 24. You're there for that.

For FOH manager, Simona posted something and got some responses, but you haven't seen any candidate profiles or details.

---

**The vendors and partners.**

Nomeda at Brand Me handles physical branding and print design. She's ready to go, waiting on your approvals to start. Deimantė does exterior signage — she quoted €2,250 + VAT plus €320 + VAT for installation, including handling municipality permits. Rolandas decided to check an alternative through his BNI business club contact instead. No update on that. Audronė at Verslo Struktūra handles DSS and accounting services — her contract has been floating since February 26 and it's unclear if it's signed. Karolina does interior design — she sent stone samples for the bar (quartz brown at €135 per square meter, a nicer alternative at €200, slab about 5 square meters) and confirmed a deal with furniture maker Kubilius for bar finishing. No response from owners on that. Jokado does tableware — new account manager, proposal being recalculated, you're visiting to choose napkin models.

---

**The communication problem.**

This is the single biggest operational risk on the project, and it's been documented repeatedly. The pattern is: you send emails with detailed deliverables and questions → no reply for one to two weeks → you have to call → you get partial verbal information → you agree to meet → the meeting gets postponed or cancelled → the cycle restarts.

This has been going on since at least January. You've documented it formally in multiple emails. The clearest consequence was nearly losing Andrius — a real chef candidate, gone because nobody replied to his email for a week. That's the preview of what happens with future staff, vendors, and eventually guests.

You sent a major status email on March 3 covering over ten items. Then another on March 5 pushing DSS and signage. Then menus and signage drafts on March 6. You called Simona on March 10, went through everything verbal
